QAPair.Question: What is (are) Acromegaly ?
QAPair.Answer: Acromegaly is a hormonal disorder that results from too much growth hormone (GH) in the body. Usually the excess GH comes from benign pituitary adenomas. It is most often diagnosed in middle-aged adults and can cause serious cardiovascular, metabolic, joint, and colon complications if untreated.

QAPair.Question: What are the symptoms of Acromegaly ?
QAPair.Answer: Common manifestations include enlargement and swelling of the hands and feet, changes in ring or shoe size, protrusion of the brow and lower jaw, enlarged nose and spaced teeth. Other symptoms include joint aches, thick oily skin, skin tags, enlarged lips and tongue, deepened voice, sleep apnea, sweating, fatigue, headaches, impaired vision, menstrual abnormalities, erectile dysfunction, and decreased libido.

QAPair.Question: What causes Acromegaly ?
QAPair.Answer: Acromegaly is caused by prolonged overproduction of growth hormone. In more than 95 percent of affected people, a benign pituitary adenoma produces excess GH. Rarely, tumors elsewhere produce GH or growth hormone-releasing hormone. Excess GH raises insulin-like growth factor I, causing bone overgrowth and organ enlargement.

QAPair.Question: How many people are affected by Acromegaly ?
QAPair.Answer: Scientists estimate that three to four out of every million people develop acromegaly each year and about 60 out of every million people have the disease at any time. The frequency may be underestimated because diagnosis is often missed.

QAPair.Question: How to diagnose Acromegaly ?
QAPair.Answer: Diagnosis uses blood testing and imaging. The oral glucose tolerance test is used because glucose normally suppresses GH, while suppression does not occur with GH overproduction. IGF-I is a more stable screening measure. After biochemical diagnosis, pituitary MRI is used to locate and measure the tumor; CT can be used when MRI is unsuitable.

QAPair.Question: What are the treatments for Acromegaly ?
QAPair.Answer: Treatment options include surgical removal of the tumor, medical therapy, and pituitary radiation therapy. Transsphenoidal surgery is the first option for many patients. Medicines include somatostatin analogs and other agents that lower GH or block its effects. Treatment aims to normalize hormone production, relieve pressure, preserve pituitary function, and improve symptoms.
